title: Check
questions.0.text: A function works out a total and ends with console.log(sum). Then it is called: const total = orderTotal(4, "small"); What is total?
questions.0.options.0: The number you can see in the console
questions.0.options.3: The code crashes because the function does not return
questions.0.why: A function with no return always returns undefined. The number reached your eyes, but not the code. That is exactly why it looks like everything works.
questions.1.text: Inside an if, const discount = 0.1 is declared. Two lines below, outside the curly brackets, discount is read. What happens?
questions.1.options.0: ReferenceError, the name does not exist outside
questions.1.options.2: The value is 0.1, if the condition was true
questions.1.why: const and let live only until the closing bracket of their own block. Outside, the name is not empty and not undefined — it simply is not there. That is why this mistake crashes, and why it is one of the easy ones.
questions.2.text: const twice = (n) => { n * 2; }; Then console.log(twice(5)); What comes out?
questions.2.options.1: Nothing, the line does not run
questions.2.options.2: SyntaxError because of the curly brackets
questions.2.why: An arrow straight to an expression returns that expression. An arrow to curly brackets is a body — return has to be written inside. The sum is worked out and thrown away. No errors, no warnings.
questions.3.text: A function starts with tip = tip || 0.1. A customer explicitly says no tip and it is called with 0. What tip is charged?
questions.3.options.3: It depends on the size of the bill
questions.3.why: || asks whether the value is truthy, not whether it was passed. Zero is not truthy and gets replaced silently. A default in the function's brackets only kicks in on undefined and lets zero through untouched.
questions.4.text: A function adds its result to a running total outside itself and returns that total. You call it twice with the same numbers. Which is true?
questions.4.options.0: The two answers are the same
questions.4.options.3: The second answer is bigger, because the function remembers the first
questions.4.why: The answer depends on history, not on the numbers passed in. Such a function cannot be checked in one line and cannot be called a second time without care. A pure one gives the same output for the same input and touches nothing outside.
